focus_ar: اندماج NG — دمج /n/ + /k/ أو /g/ في صوت /ŋ/
arabic_challenge: In words like 'think' and 'finger', the /n/ sound before /k/ or /g/ merges into a single nasal sound /ŋ/ (the 'ng' sound, as in 'sing') produced at the back of the mouth — it does NOT stay as a separate, front-of-mouth /n/ followed by a distinct /k//g/. Arabic speakers, influenced by spelling (which shows 'n' and 'k'/'g' as separate letters), often keep them fully separate, producing an extra, non-native-sounding /n/ before the /k/ or /g/.

isolation.instructions: Before /k/, the /n/ sound is produced at the BACK of the mouth (like the 'ng' in 'sing'), not the front — they blend into one continuous back-of-mouth motion, not two separate sounds.
isolation.instructions_ar: قبل /k/، صوت /n/ بينطق من مؤخرة الفم (زي ng في sing)، مش من قدام — بيندمجوا في حركة واحدة متصلة من مؤخرة الفم، مش صوتين منفصلين.

sentence_practice: I think the bank needs a pink sink.
Thank you for the drink — I'll sink into this chair.
My uncle threw away the junk near the bank.
daily_drills.record_this: I think the bank needs a pink sink — thank you for the drink, I'll sink into this chair while my uncle sorts through the junk.
daily_drills.self_check: Does the /n/ before /k/ feel like it's produced at the BACK of your mouth, blending smoothly into the /k/, rather than a separate front-of-mouth /n/?

isolation.instructions: The same back-of-mouth merging happens before /g/ in words like 'finger' and 'angle' — the /n/ and /g/ blend into one continuous motion.
isolation.instructions_ar: نفس الدمج من مؤخرة الفم بيحصل قبل /g/ في كلمات زي finger وangle — الـ /n/ و/g/ بيندمجوا في حركة واحدة متصلة.

sentence_practice: My finger points at a single angle.
England has an interesting language and history.
Hunger made him point his finger at the food.
daily_drills.record_this: My finger points at a single angle — England has an interesting language, and hunger made him point at the food.
daily_drills.self_check: For 'finger' and 'angle', is the transition from /n/ to /g/ smooth and back-of-mouth, not choppy or front-of-mouth?

target_sounds: contrast: NG-coalescence vs. genuinely separate /n/+/k/ across word boundaries

isolation.instructions: NG-coalescence happens WITHIN a single word or morpheme. When 'n' and 'k'/'g' belong to two SEPARATE words, they do NOT merge this way — they stay as a genuinely separate /n/ followed by /k/ or /g/.
isolation.instructions_ar: دمج NG بيحصل جوه كلمة واحدة أو جزء صرفي واحد. لما n وk/g يكونوا في كلمتين منفصلتين، ما بيندمجوش كده — بيفضلوا /n/ منفصلة متبوعة بـ /k/ أو /g/.
practice: think (one word, merged ŋk) vs. one cat (two words, separate n+k)
finger (one word, merged ŋg) vs. one goat (two words, separate n+g)

sentence_practice: I think one cat is enough for our bank of pets.
My finger points at one goat near the fence.
I saw one kite while I sat by the bank.
daily_drills.record_this: I think one cat is enough — my finger points at one goat near the bank, where I also saw one kite.
daily_drills.self_check: Could you correctly distinguish the merged NG sound (within a word) from a genuinely separate n+k/g (across two words)?

target_sounds: NG-coalescence in academic and everyday high-frequency vocabulary

isolation.instructions: This pattern appears in extremely common vocabulary you'll use constantly — practice it in a wider range of everyday words.
isolation.instructions_ar: هذا النمط بيظهر في مفردات شائعة جدًا هتستخدمها كتير — تدرب عليه في نطاق أوسع من الكلمات اليومية.
practice: thanks, banking, thinking, drinking, English, singer, longer, stronger

sentence_practice: Thanks for thinking about the English banking system.
The singer is getting stronger and longer with practice.
I'm thinking about drinking less and banking more.
daily_drills.record_this: Thanks for thinking about the English banking system — the singer is getting stronger and longer with practice, and I'm thinking about drinking less.
daily_drills.self_check: In this wider vocabulary set, did the NG-coalescence pattern hold consistently across all the words?

isolation.instructions: Apply this pattern while delivering an analytical statement, since 'thinking', 'banking' and similar words appear often in L2's analysis-style speaking missions.
isolation.instructions_ar: طبّق النمط ده وانت بتقدم تصريح تحليلي، لأن كلمات زي thinking وbanking بتظهر كتير في مهام الكلام التحليلية في المستوى الثاني.
practice: Deliver Day 4's sentences in an analytical, presentation-style tone.

sentence_practice: When thinking analytically about English banking, we see stronger, longer-term trends.
I'm thinking the singer's stronger performance shows longer preparation.
Thanks to thinking clearly, our banking strategy is stronger.
daily_drills.record_this: When thinking analytically about English banking, we see stronger, longer-term trends — thanks to clear thinking, our strategy is stronger.
daily_drills.self_check: At analytical/presentation speed, did the NG-coalescence pattern remain natural and consistent?

daily_drills.target_sounds: Review: NG-coalescence with /k/, with /g/, contrast with cross-word separation, wider vocabulary, presentation speed
challenge_sentences: I think one cat and one goat are enough — my finger points at the bank, where I'm thinking about English banking.
Thanks for thinking about the singer's stronger, longer performance near the bank.
One kite, one cat, and my finger all point toward the English bank I'm thinking about.
daily_drills.record_this: I think one cat and one goat are enough — my finger points at the bank, where I'm thinking about English banking and a singer's stronger, longer performance.
daily_drills.self_check: Mark every merged NG sound and every genuinely separate n+k/g across word boundaries. Did you distinguish them correctly?

test_yourself.instructions: Record this passage naturally, correctly merging NG-coalescence within words while keeping genuinely separate n+k/g across word boundaries.
test_yourself.passage: I'm thinking about my banking habits these days. Thanks to some careful thinking, I've realized that drinking less coffee and saving more could make my finances stronger. My uncle, a singer with a strong voice, told me that one small change, like one cup less a day, can add up over a longer period. English financial habits are interesting to think about, and I hope my thinking leads to a stronger, longer-term plan.

scoring_guide.90_100: NG-coalescence correctly and consistently merged within words, correctly kept separate across word boundaries, at natural speed.
scoring_guide.70_89: Mostly correct; occasional over-separation within a word.
scoring_guide.50_69: Merging present only in heavily drilled words.
scoring_guide.below_50: Consistent separation of n+k/g throughout — go back and repeat Days 1-4, focusing on the back-of-mouth tongue position.

for_arabic_speakers: This is Blueprint Table 19's explicit L2 Week 7 assignment (NG-coalescence).

A useful trick: practice the standalone 'ng' sound in 'sing' first (hum it, feeling the back-of-mouth nasal resonance), then attach a /k/ or /g/ right after that same tongue position, without moving your tongue forward first.
This completes the trio of L2 phoneme-merging patterns (Yod-dropping Week 3, Dark L Week 5, NG-coalescence Week 7) that Table 19 assigns specifically to this level.
supplementary_notes.recording_requirement: Record the 'record_this' sentence every day. By Day 7, this should feel like a natural extension of your growing back-of-mouth articulation control (also used in Week 5's Dark L).
